That was the judgment of his hypochondriac ignorance, which was only right on the point of infertility 芒聙?and in this, of course, completely. The diversion lasted for weeks. Sometimes it seemed to do harm - which of course had to be based on error - and sometimes to help, which then also turned out to be an error. The result was zero, without being named and expressly proclaimed. The venture fizzled, and Hans Castorp continued to play solitaire face-to-face with the demon whose rampant rule he felt was ending in terror.

text1: Siddhartha didn't answer. He was not very curious about the teaching, he did not believe that it would teach him anything new, since he, like Govinda, had heard the content of this Buddha teaching again and again, albeit from second- and third-hand reports. But he looked attentively at Gotama's head, at his shoulders, at his feet, at his still hanging hand, and it seemed to him that every limb on every finger of this hand was teaching, spoke, breathed, smelled, shone truth. text1: If you saw me, my dear, in the torrent of distraction! How parched my senses become! Not a moment of fullness of heart, not a happy hour! Nothing! Nothing! I stand in front of a rarity box and see the males and little horses moving around in front of me, and I often ask myself whether it isn't optical fraud. I play along, or rather, am played like a marionette, sometimes grabbing my neighbor's wooden hand and shuddering. In the evening I resolve to enjoy the sunrise and cannot get out of bed; during the day I hope to enjoy the moonlight and stay in my room. I don't really know why I get up, why I go to sleep.

text1: The examining magistrate shifted in his chair, either out of embarrassment or impatience. The man behind him, with whom he had already been talking, leaned over again, either to give him general encouragement or to give him some special advice. Down below, the people were talking quietly but animatedly. The two parties, who had seemed to have such opposite opinions, were now mixing together, and some people were pointing at K., while others were pointing at the examining magistrate. The foggy mist in the room was extremely annoying, and it even prevented a closer observation of those who were standing farther away. It must have been particularly annoying for the gallery visitors, who were forced to ask quiet questions of the people around them in order to get more information, but doing so with furtive sidelong glances at the examining magistrate. The answers were given just as quietly, protected by hands held in front of them.

text1: This legal proceeding completed the despair of Candide. In fact he had experienced miseries a thousand times more painful, but the coldness of the judge, and that of the merchant who had robbed him, roused his bile and plunged him into a black melancholy. The malice of men rose up before his spirit in all its ugliness, and his mind dwelt only on gloomy thoughts. Finally, when a French vessel was ready to leave for Bordeaux, since he had no more diamond-laden sheep to transport, he took a cabin at a fair price, and made it known in the town that he would pay passage and keep, plus two thousand piastres, to any honest man who wanted to make the journey with him, on condition that this man must be the most disgusted with his own condition and the most unhappy man in the province.

commet: I think that both translations here are pretty bad. They both contain sentences that were translated too literally, and they both contain errors. Ultimately, I selected the second translation because the first one includes a serious error: it reads "[...] he would pay passage and keep, plus two thousand piastres, to any honest man [...]" This is supposed to be "he would pay passage and GIVE two thousand piastres to any honest man [...]" This was a very simple thing to translate. I am very puzzled by this mistranslation.

text1: "My friends," he used to say, "I don't ask you to imitate the English racers, who lose eighteen pounds after two days' training, and twenty-five after five days, but we ought to do something to get into the best possible condition for a long journey. commet: The translation I selected is much more idiomatic and does not stick to French syntax. The other one is a very close literal translation and the result is that some of it does not make sense / sounds incredibly awkward in English. For example, "[...] such a one who before training could not run a mile [...]" - I'm pretty sure the expression "such a one" does not exist in English. The good translation has this part as "[...] someone who before training could not run the space of a mile [...]" where "someone" makes a lot more sense. There are other oddities in the text I did not select. In this text, the French verb "disait" at the beginning is conjugated in the imperfect tense. The imperfect can be translated into English either as a preterit or as "used to". In this context, the preterit is clearly the obvious choice as we are not dealing with a past habitual action. Yet, the bad translation has it as " "My friends," he used to say, "I don't ask you [...]"

For example, "Candide [...] fut surpris de la beaut脙漏 des tableaux" was mistranslated as "Candide [...] was amazed at the beauty of the pictures". You can be surprised by something but not necessarily amazed by it. In other words, the bad translation adds meaning that shouldn't be added. At last the brig found a pass and advanced painfully through it; often an enormous icicle paralyzed its course for long hours; the fog obstructed the pilot's view; as long as one sees a mile ahead, one can easily parry obstacles; but in the midst of these misty whirlwinds, the view often stopped at less than a cable's length. The very strong swell was tiring.
loser_text: On Thursday, 26 July the Forward hugged Dundas Island, and then veered north; but she soon found herself facing an ice shelf, eight or nine feet high, made up of small icebergs torn from the coast; so for a long time she was obliged to continue curving westwards. The continuous cracking of the ice added to the groans of the ship, to produce a sad sound, resembling sighs and complaints. Finally the brig found a pass and struggled into it; often an enormous ice floe would paralyse her route for long hours; the fog restricted the pilot芒聙聶s view; provided he could see a mile ahead, obstacles could easily be avoided; but in the midst of the flurries, vision was often limited to less than a cable. The strong swell was tiring.
commet: The translation I selected is much more idiomatic and doesn't stick to French syntax. The other translation has some very awkward phrases that do not sound English. For example, the date: "On Thursday, 26 July the Forward [...]" was literally copied from French. Something else that I found odd is the misinterpretation of both the subject pronoun "il" and the possessive pronoun "sa" (it can mean his, her, or its depending on who or what the "possessor" is). At some point, "il" (=he) is translated as "she" even though "il" is clearly masculine. "[...] il fut oblig脙漏 d芒聙聶en prolonger longtemps la courbure [...]" was mistranslated as "[...] she was obliged to continue curving [...]". The possessive pronoun "sa" refers to the brig (=the ship) so it must be translated as "its" in English since the ship is inanimate. The bad translation has it as "her" but I don't think it's because the author thought it'd be a good idea to add a personification here. I'm pretty sure it's just an error.

commet: I think both translations are OK even though I believe they both misinterpreted the part "[...] et que la bonne avait manqu脙漏 [...]". In that sentence, the relative pronoun "que" refers, in my opinion, to "the dish" and not to "the name". Both translations took it to refer to "the name" and this results in a mistranslation of the whole sentence. I selected the first translation because the second translation contains an error. Take the French "Elle vit 脙 Rouen des dames qui portaient 脙 leur montre [...]" - In the second translation, you can read: "She saw ladies at Rouen who wore a bunch of trinkets on their WATCH [...]" and in the first translation: "She saw ladies at Rouen who wore a bundle of trinkets on their WATCHES [...]" In French, when one person has one thing (a watch, a head, a mouth, an umbrella, etc.) we'll use the singular even though there are several people and therefore several watches, heads, etc. What matters in French in terms of agreement is the number of a particular item PER PERSON. In English, however, it's the opposite. What matters is the number of a particular item TOTAL. For example, in English you'll say "The men put their HATS on" where "hats" is plural even though each man only has one hat. The translation I selected correctly used "watches" in the plural. However, the translation I did not select kept the singular on "watch" as we do in French, which is incorrect in English.

commet: Both translations are very good. I chose the second one because the first took too much liberty erasing a subject. Consider the French sentence: "Hatteras fit monter sur le pont une grande quantit脙漏 de provisions, les effets de campement [...]" which roughly translates as "Hatteras had x and x brought on deck". The first translation erases Hatteras as the person who orders things to be brought on board in "[...] a large quantity of provisions, camping equipment, and blankets for the crew were brought on deck". The second text (the one I chose) did not erase Hatteras in "Hatteras had a large quantity of provisions, camp equipage, clothes, and blankets brought on deck". While I understand that sometimes it is best to delete things, the deletion here was not necessary. In fact, I would say that it may be detrimental to the story. In addition, the second text (the one I chose) did a good thing deleting the extra information "(-14脗掳 centigrades)" found in the French text. Since Farenheit is used in the story, it must be "translated" in celsius for a French readership. However, when translating into English, it would be very strange to indicate what the temperature is in F and then give an English-speaking readership the celsius equivalent since the C equivalent won't be helpful for anyone in English.

I also did not like that the first translation uses the feminine pronoun "she" to refer to the ship. In French, the gender used to refer to the ship is masculine because "ship" / "boat" is masculine, but it does not personify the boat. We just don't have a neutral gender in French. I think that in English the boat should be referred to as "it". I understand that sometimes people will use "she" to refer to boats or cars, because they are obsessed with their vehicles, but it is not the impression that I am getting here.

commet: I think both translations are generally quite good. I chose the second translation because the first one contains some errors and oddities, described below. 1) For some reason, Text 1 chose not to translate titles (e.g., Duke, Count/Countess, etc.) even though these titles have an equivalent in English. Text 1 did not translate the following titles: Comte, Comtesse, Duc, Duchesse. The second text translated those correctly into Count, Countess, Duke, Duchess. 2) Whenever the French word "duc" (=Duke) appeared, Text 1 translated it as "due" with an -e at the end. This doesn't make sense given the context. It looks like a machine translated it and accidentally processed letter -c as if it were letter -e in the word "duc". 3) In the French "Quelques parents de Mme Walter [...]" - the "Mme" is an abbreviation for "Madame". It should be translated as "Mrs." in English. In some cases it can be translated as "Madam" or "Madame". However, keeping the original "Mme" would not make sense to an English-speaking reader.

commet: Both translations are good. I chose the first one because it translated the multiple occurrences of 3rd person sg pronouns referring to the animal as "it" / "its". The second text translated all these occurrences as "he" / "him" / "his". Given the context, I think "it" is much more appropriate. I think that "he" / "she" is only appropriate in English when the animal is a pet and/or when an affectionate relationship between the animal and the human has been established. Here, it's quite the opposite. The human is doing his best to capture/kill the animal in order to use its "parts" (skin, teeth, etc.)

text1: A moment later, however, he suddenly began to look around him quickly and anxiously; this first vision might be the harbinger and predecessor of a second vision. That must be certain. Had he really forgotten about a possible encounter when they set off for the park? To be sure, when he entered the park he seemed to be quite unaware that he was going there - such was his state of mind. If he had been able to be more attentive, he would have noticed a quarter of an hour earlier that Aglaya was also looking around her, apparently in some unease, from time to time, as though she were searching for something. Now that his own unease had become very noticeable, Aglaya芒聙聶s agitation and anxiety had increased, and as soon as he looked back, she too looked round. The resolution of their anxious feelings soon ensued.

text1: At that time I could not understand anything from this thieves' conversation, but afterward I guessed that it was about the affairs of the Yaitsky army, which at that time had just been pacified after the riot of 1772. Savelich listened with an air of great displeasure. He glanced suspiciously first at the owner, then at the counselor. The inn, or, according to the locals, umet, was located aside, in the steppe, far from any village, and very much resembled a robber's wharf. But there was nothing to be done. It was impossible to think about continuing the path. Savelich's uneasiness amused me greatly. In the meantime, I settled down for the night and lay down on a bench. Savelich decided to get out on the stove; the owner lay down on the floor. Soon the whole hut was snoring, and I fell asleep like the dead.

text1: And I remembered the whole course of my mental labors and I was horrified. It was now clear to me that for a man to be able to live he either had not to see the infinite or have an explanation of the meaning of life in which the finite was equated with the infinite. I had such an explanation but I had no need for it while I believed in the finite, and I began to test it by reason. And with the light of reason I found the whole of my previous explanation to dissolve in dust. But there came a time when I stopped believing in the finite. And then I began to construct out of what I knew, on rational foundations, an explanation that would give the meaning of life; but nothing got constructed. Together with mankind芒聙聶s best minds I came to 0 = 0 and was very surprised to get such a solution when nothing else could come of it.

First of all, there should be a present time reference in the second sentence of the text as it is in the second translation, while the author of the first translation makes mistakes in choosing "he either had not to see the infinite" and "the finite was equated with the infinite" which makes it impossible to figure out the meaning of the sentence. text1: The prince really looked at her very much at that moment, noticing that she again began to blush terribly. On such occasions, the more she blushed, the more she seemed to be angry with herself for it, which was evidently expressed in her sparkling eyes; usually a minute later she was already transferring her anger to the one with whom she spoke, whether he was guilty or not, and began to quarrel with him. Knowing and feeling her savagery and bashfulness, she usually entered little into the conversation and was more silent than the other sisters, sometimes even too silent. When, especially in such delicate cases, it was absolutely necessary to speak, she began the conversation with unusual arrogance and, as it were, with some kind of challenge. She always had a presentiment in advance when she began or wanted to begin to blush.
text2: The prince was indeed peering at her intently at that moment, noticing that she had again begun to blush terribly. On such occasions, the more she blushed, the more she seemed to be angry with herself for it, as showed clearly in her flashing eyes; usually she would transfer her wrath a moment later to the one she was talking with, whether or not it was his fault, and begin to quarrel with him. Knowing and feeling her wildness and shyness, she usually entered little into conversation and was more taciturn than the other sisters, sometimes even much too taciturn. When, especially on such ticklish occasions, she absolutely had to speak, she would begin the conversation with an extraordinary haughtiness and as if with a sort of defiance. She always felt beforehand when she was beginning or about to begin to blush.

commet: The first translation is better. Grammar constructions used here are the only possible ways to translate repetitive action in the past ("usually she would transfer her wrath..." and "she would begin the conversation"). I also like the word choice in the first translation: "the prince was indeed peering at her" is the exact equivalent of the Russian phrase and conveys the meaning better than "The prince really looked at her". The exact same thing with the phrase "when she began or wanted to begin to blush" which is a perfect translation of the Russian phrase while "when she was beginning or about to begin to blush" in the second translation doesn't convey the sense of the initial sentence.

text1: The departure of a Soviet airplane of a new design was to take place. Babichev was invited. The guests went beyond the barrier. Babichev also dominated this chosen society. As soon as he entered into a conversation with someone, a circle closed around him. Everyone listened to him with respectful attention. He showed off in his gray suit, grandiose, above all shoulders, an arch of the shoulders. Black binoculars hung from straps on his stomach. Listening to the interlocutor, he put his hands in his pockets and quietly swayed on widely spaced legs from heel to toe and from toe to heel. He often scratches his nose. After scratching, he looks at his fingers, pinched together and close to his eyes. Listeners, like schoolchildren, involuntarily repeat his movements and the play of his face. They also scratch their noses, surprised at themselves.
text2: The take-off of a newly designed Soviet plane was supposed to take place. They invited Babichev. The guests went out beyond the barrier. Babichev predominated even in this select society. He had only to join with someone in conversation when a circle closed around him. Everyone listened to him with respectful attention. He stood out in his gray suit, grandiose, with his shoulders, the arch of his shoulders, higher than everyone. Black binoculars hung on straps at his stomach. Listening to the person talking to him, he put his hands into his pockets and rocked quietly on his wide-spread legs from heel to toe and toe to heel. He often scratches his nose. Having scratched, he looks at his fingers which are in pinch formation and brought close to his eyes. His listeners, like school children, involuntarily repeat his motions and the play of his face. They also scratch their noses, wondering at themselves.

commet: Both translations are almost equally good, but I prefer the first one. The second sentence in the text requires a passive construction, which can be seen in the first translation. text1: The enormous dial at the pinnacle of the Tower was a face: bending over through the clouds and spitting down seconds, waiting indifferently. And at exactly 13:06, a commotion occurred in the quadrilateral. All this was quite close to where I stood; I could see the minute details and I clearly remember a thin, long neck and the tangled mess of light blue veins on his temples, like rivers on the geographical map of a small, unknown world, and this unknown world was a young cipher by the looks of him. He must have noticed someone in our row: he got up on his tiptoes, extended his neck, and stopped. One of the guards cracked him with the bluish spark of an electrical whip; he yelped thinly, like a puppy. And then there was a distinct crack, approximately every two seconds芒聙?a yelp芒聙聰a crack芒聙聰a yelp.

text1: Even at the beginning, as soon as the prince entered the drawing room, he sat down as far as possible from the Chinese vase, with which Aglaya had so frightened him. Is it possible to believe that after Aglaya's words yesterday, some indelible conviction came into him, some amazing and impossible premonition that he would certainly break this vase tomorrow, no matter how he avoided it, no matter how he avoided trouble? But it was. In the course of the evening other strong but bright impressions began to flood into his soul; we already talked about this. He forgot his premonition. When he heard about Pavlishchev and Ivan Fyodorovich let him down and showed him again to Ivan Petrovich, he moved closer to the table and fell straight into an armchair beside a huge, beautiful Chinese vase, which stood on a pedestal, almost next to his elbow, a little behind.

commet: These two translations are not perfect at all, they are almost equal and there is only one big difference between them - a mistake made in the second translation. That's why I've chosen the first one. According to the second translation, a bid Chinese vase frightened Prince: "the Chinese vase, which had so frightened him". It is a mistake because according to the source text it was another character, Aglaya, who had frightened Prince by telling him about the vase

text1: The prince noticed the sweet, affectionate look of Vera Lebedeva, who was also in a hurry to make her way to him through the crowd. Past all, he extended his hand to her first; she flushed with pleasure and wished him "a happy life from this very day." Then she ran headlong into the kitchen; there she prepared a snack; but even before the prince's arrival - she could only just look away from her work for a minute - she would appear on the terrace and listen with all her might to heated disputes about the most abstract and strange things for her, which did not stop between drunken guests. Her younger sister, who opened her mouth, fell asleep in the next room, on a chest, but the boy, Lebedev's son, stood beside Kolya and Ippolit, and one look at his animated face showed that he was ready to stand here in one place, enjoying and listening, at least for a while longer. ten hours in a row.

commet: I believe that the first translation is better than the second one for the following reasons: 1) Vera Lebedeva is one of the characters mentioned in this passage. The author of the second translation makes a mistake and writes her name as "Lebedev" which is a male form of the last name. 2) I think that the phrase "strange things for her, which did not cease among the tipsy guests" in the second translation isn't as accurate as the exact phrase in the first translation 3) According to the text it is clear that it's correct to say "he was ready to stand here" as in the first translation but not "he was ready to stand there" as in the second one.

text1: But it turned out that you are just a dignitary, ignorant and stupid, like all the dignitaries who were before you and will be after you. And, like all dignitaries, you are a tyrant. Only tyranny can explain the hurricane you raised around a piece of mediocre sausage, or the fact that you brought an unknown young man from the street to your place. And, perhaps, out of the same tyranny, you brought Volodya Makarov closer to you, about whom I know only one thing, that he is a football player. You are a bard. You need jesters and freeloaders. I have no doubt that that Volodya Makarov ran away from you, unable to endure bullying. Like me, you must have systematically turned him into a fool.

I believe that the usage of Past Simple and Future Simple (as in the first translation) conveys the meaning of the source text better. 2) The exact same thing with the second translation. text1: But a minute later, he suddenly began to look around quickly and restlessly: this first vision could be a harbinger and precursor of the second vision. It should have been probably. Had he forgotten about a possible meeting when they went to the station? True, when he went to the station, it seems that he did not know at all that he was going here - in such a state he was. If he had known or could have been more attentive, he might have noticed a quarter of an hour ago that Aglaya from time to time, and also, as it were, glanced around anxiously, also seemed to be looking for something around her. Now that his uneasiness had become very noticeable, Aglaya's excitement and uneasiness increased, and as soon as he looked back, she looked back almost at once. The resolution of the alarm soon followed.

text1: I did not think so then, but the germs of these thoughts were already in me. I understood, 1) that my position with Schopenhauer and Solomon, despite our wisdom, is stupid: we understand that life is evil, and yet we live. This is obviously stupid, because if life is stupid - and I love everything reasonable so much - then life must be destroyed, and there will be no one to deny it. 2) I understood that all our reasoning revolves in a vicious circle, like a wheel that does not cling to a gear. No matter how much and no matter how well we argue, we cannot get an answer to the question, and it will always be 0=0, and that is why our path is probably erroneous. 3) I began to understand that the answers given by faith contain the deepest wisdom of mankind, and that I had no right to deny them on the basis of reason, and that, most importantly, these answers alone answer the question of life.

text1: In the evening, at home, he sits, shaded by the palm greens of the lampshade. In front of him are sheets of paper, notebooks, small sheets of paper with columns of numbers. He flips the pages of the desk calendar, jumps up, looks in the bookcase, takes out packs, kneels on a chair and, with his stomach on the table, propping his fat face in his hands, reads. The green area of the table is covered with a glass plate. After all, what is special? A man works, a man is at home, in the evening, he works. The man, staring at the sheet, picks his ear with a pencil. Nothing special. But all his behavior says: you are an inhabitant, Kavalerov. Of course, he does not state this. There must be nothing like it in his mind. But it is clear without words. Someone else tells me about it. Someone else makes me mad while I'm watching him.

text1: But Ganya had already endured too much that day and that evening, and he was not prepared for this last unexpected test. The crowd parted before them into two halves, and he remained eye to eye with Nastasya Filippovna, three paces away from her. She stood right by the fireplace and waited, not taking her eyes off him, fiery, intently. Ganya, in a tailcoat, with a hat in his hand and with gloves, stood in front of her silently and without answer, arms folded and looking at the fire. An insane smile roamed across his face as pale as a handkerchief. True, he could not take his eyes off the fire, from the smoldering pack; but it seemed that something new had entered his soul; as if he had sworn to endure torture; he didn't move; after a few moments it became clear to everyone that he would not go for a pack, he did not want to go.

She stood by the fireplace and waited, not taking her fiery, intent gaze off him. Ganya, in his tailcoat, with his hat in his hand and his gloves on, stood before her silently and without reply, arms folded, looking at the fire. An insane smile wandered over his face, as pale as a handkerchief. commet: The first translation is better than the second one for the following reasons: 1) The phrase "Ganya, in his tailcoat, with his hat in his hand and his gloves on" in the second contains too many "his", there aren't as many pronouns even in the source text 2) According to the source text the last sentence should inform readers that the character "didn't want to go" as in the first translation. The phrase "he would not go for the logs, would not go" in the second translation doesn't convey this meaning

text1: He did not finish; Ferdyshchenko quickly put a chair behind him, and the general, somewhat weak on his feet that afternoon, flopped down, or rather, fell on the chair, but this, however, did not embarrass him. He sat down directly opposite Nastasya Filippovna and with a pleasant grin, slowly and effectively, raised her fingers to his lips. In general, it was rather difficult to embarrass the general. His outward appearance, besides some slovenliness, was still quite decent, which he himself knew very well. He had happened before to be in very good society, from which he was finally excluded only two or three years ago. From the same period, he indulged too much without restraint in some of his weaknesses; but the dexterous and pleasant manner remained in him hitherto. Nastasya Filippovna seemed to be overjoyed at the appearance of Ardalion Alexandrovich, whom, of course, she knew from hearsay.

commet: Both translations aren't quite good at conveying the meaning of the source text but the first translation is a little bit better than the second one The author of the second translation overuses Past Perfect when it is absolutely no need. The sentence "He had happened before to be in very good society, from which he was finally excluded only two or three years ago" is an example of the right usage of past tenses (taken from the first translation) and the only need to use Past Perfect in this passage

text1: Lebedev drew himself up. It is true that he was sometimes even too naive and importunate in his curiosity; but at the same time he was a rather cunning and sinuous person, and in some cases even too insidiously silent; By continuous repulsions, the prince almost prepared an enemy for himself in him. But the prince repulsed him not because he despised him, but because the topic of his curiosity was delicate. Several days ago, the prince looked at some of his dreams as a crime, but Lukyan Timofeich took the prince芒聙聶s refusals for nothing more than personal disgust and distrust towards himself, went away with a wounded heart and was jealous of the prince, not only Kolya and Keller, but even his own daughter. , Vera Lukyanovna. Even at that very moment, perhaps, he could and wished to sincerely inform the prince of one piece of news that was extremely interesting for the prince, but he gloomily fell silent and did not tell him.

Even at that very moment he might have wished to tell the prince of something extremely interesting to the prince, but he fell silent gloomily and did not tell him.

commet: Both translations contain a mistake in the second-to-last sentence. An accurate translation should sound as "and was jealous not only of Kolya and Keller, but even of his own daughter Vera Lukyanovna". The first translation is still better than the second one. 1) I don't like the overuse of the pronouns in the phrase "the prince had almost prepared an enemy for himself in him by his continual rebuffs" in the second translation. They make it difficult for readers to get the actual meaning of the sentence. 2) According to the source text there is no need to use Past Perfect in this sentence: "But the prince had rebuffed him not because he despised him, but because the subject of his curiosity was a delicate one" in the second translation.

text1: The prince finally felt not exactly sorry, but as if ashamed. The thought even flashed through his mind: "Couldn't something be made out of this man by someone's good influence?" He considered his own influence, for some reason, very worthless, not from self-deprecation, but from a certain special view of things. Little by little they began to talk, and to the point that they did not want to disperse. Keller confessed to such cases with unusual readiness that it was impossible to imagine how one could talk about such cases. Starting each story, he assured positively that he was repenting and inwardly 芒聙聹full of tears,芒聙?and meanwhile he told it as if he was proud of the deed, and at the same time it was sometimes so funny that he and the prince finally laughed like crazy.
text2: The prince began to feel, finally, not exactly sorry, but somehow as if he were guilty. text1: At that time I could not understand anything from this thieves' conversation, but afterward I guessed that it was about the affairs of the Yaitsky army, which at that time had just been pacified after the riot of 1772. Savelich listened with an air of great displeasure. He glanced suspiciously first at the owner, then at the counselor. The inn, or, according to the locals, umet, was located aside, in the steppe, far from any village, and very much resembled a robber's wharf. But there was nothing to be done. It was impossible to think about continuing the path. Savelich's uneasiness amused me greatly. In the meantime, I settled down for the night and lay down on a bench. Savelich decided to get out on the stove; the owner lay down on the floor. Soon the whole hut was snoring, and I fell asleep like the dead.
text2: I could not make head or tail of this conversation between the innkeeper and the driver, but later I guessed that they were talking about the Yaitsk army, which had just been pacified after the mutiny of 1772. Savelich listened with an air of great displeasure. He looked suspiciously first at the innkeeper, then at the driver. The standing army, or, as they called it, the umyt, was stationed in the steppe, far from any village, and very much resembled a bandit's lair. But there was nothing to be done. It was impossible to think of going on. Savelich's uneasiness amused me greatly. Meanwhile I made myself comfortable for the night and lay down on the bench. Savelich decided to sleep on the stove; the innkeeper lay down on the floor. Soon the whole hut was snoring, and I fell asleep like the dead.

text1: And I remembered the whole course of my inner work and was horrified. Now it was clear to me that in order for a person to live, he must either not see the infinite, or have such an explanation of the meaning of life, in which the finite would be equated with the infinite. I had such an explanation, but it was unnecessary to me as long as I believed in the finite, and I began to test it with my mind. And in front of the light of reason, all the previous explanation shattered into dust. But the time came when I stopped believing in the finite. And then I began to build on reasonable grounds from what I knew, such an explanation that would give the meaning of life; but nothing was built. Together with the best minds of mankind, I came to the conclusion that 0=0, and I was very surprised that I got such a solution, when nothing else could come out.

text1: The departure of a Soviet airplane of a new design was to take place. Babichev was invited. The guests went beyond the barrier. Babichev also dominated this chosen society. As soon as he entered into a conversation with someone, a circle closed around him. Everyone listened to him with respectful attention. He showed off in his gray suit, grandiose, above all shoulders, an arch of the shoulders. Black binoculars hung from straps on his stomach. Listening to the interlocutor, he put his hands in his pockets and quietly swayed on widely spaced legs from heel to toe and from toe to heel. He often scratches his nose. After scratching, he looks at his fingers, pinched together and close to his eyes. Listeners, like schoolchildren, involuntarily repeat his movements and the play of his face. They also scratch their noses, surprised at themselves.
text2: There was to be a trial flight of a Soviet aeroplane of a new design. Babichev was invited. The guests went out to the barrier. Babichev was the master of the situation in this select company. As soon as he began talking to anyone, a circle formed round him. Everyone listened to him with respectful attention. He was in his grandiose best, with his shoulders above all the rest, and his chest thrown out. On his belt hung a black field-glass. While listening to his interlocutor, he would put his hands in his pockets and quietly sway on his widely-spread legs, from heel to toe, and from toe to heel. He often scratches his nose. Having done so, he looks at his fingers, which are slightly spread and brought close to his eyes. His listeners, like schoolboys, involuntarily repeat his movements and the play of his face. They also scratch their noses, surprised at themselves.

commet: The first translation is better than the second one. Let me explain why: 1) According to the source text this sentence from the second translation "He was in his grandiose best, with his shoulders above all the rest, and his chest thrown out" should contain the information about the grey suit, which is missing. Moreover, the phrase "his chest thrown out" isn't mentioned in the source text at all. text1: The stairs, on which the prince ran up from under the gate, led to the corridors of the first and second floors, along which the hotel rooms were located. This staircase, as in all houses built long ago, was of stone, dark, narrow, and wound around a thick stone pillar. On the first running platform in this column there was a recess, like a niche, no more than one step wide and half a step deep. A man, however, could fit here. Dark as it was, but, running up to the platform, the prince immediately discerned that here, in this niche, a man was hiding for some reason. The prince suddenly felt like passing by and not looking to the right. He took one step, but could not stand it and turned around.

text1: But a minute later he began to look around quickly and uneasily: this first vision might be a harbinger and precursor of a second vision. It must be so. Had he forgotten about the possible meeting when they left for the station? True, when he left for the station, he had not known at all that he was going here芒聙聰he was in such a state. If he had known or been more attentive, he could have noticed a quarter of an hour ago that Aglaya was looking around from time to time, also with some uneasiness, also apparently searching for something. Now that his uneasiness was becoming noticeable, Aglaya芒聙聶s uneasiness and agitation increased, and as soon as he looked back, she looked back almost at once. The resolution of her anxiety was soon to follow.
text2: A moment later, however, he suddenly began to look around him quickly and anxiously; this first vision might be the harbinger and predecessor of a second vision. That must be certain. Had he really forgotten about a possible encounter when they set off for the park? To be sure, when he entered the park he seemed to be quite unaware that he was going there - such was his state of mind. If he had been able to be more attentive, he would have noticed a quarter of an hour earlier that Aglaya was also looking around her, apparently in some unease, from time to time, as though she were searching for something. Now that his own unease had become very noticeable, Aglaya芒聙聶s agitation and anxiety had increased, and as soon as he looked back, she too looked round. The resolution of their anxious feelings soon ensued.

text1: As a football player Volodya was the complete opposite of Getske. Volodya was a professional sportsman, Getske was a professional player. Volodya was interested in the general course of the game, the general victory, the outcome 芒聙?Getske was interested only in showing his skill. He was an old, experienced player who did not intend to uphold the honour of the team; he valued only his own success; he was not a permanent member of any sports organization, because he had compromised himself by moving from club to club for money. He had been forbidden to take part in championship matches. He was invited only to friendly games, to exhibition matches and to trips abroad. His skill was combined with good fortune. His participation made the team dangerous. He despised the players 芒聙?both those with whom he played and the opponents. He knew that he would score against any team. The rest did not interest him. He was a hired hand.

text1: This is what I cannot understand. No matter how limited their minds may have been, they must have realized that such a life was nothing but a real massacre芒聙聰only a slow one, from day to day. The State (humanity) forbade killing a man outright and did not forbid killing millions by halves. To kill one man, that is, to reduce the sum total of human lives by fifty years, is a crime, but to reduce the sum total of human lives by fifty million years is not a crime. Isn't it absurd? With us, this mathematical-moral problem can be solved in half a minute by any ten-year-old schoolboy; but they could not芒聙聰all their Kants together (because none of the Kants guessed that a system of scientific ethics, that is, based on subtraction, addition, division, and multiplication, could be built up).
text2: Try as I may, I cannot understand it. After all, no matter how limited their intelligence, they should have understood that such a way of life was truly mass murder芒聙聰even if slow murder. The state (humaneness) forbade the killing of a single individual, but not the partial killing of millions day by day. To kill one individual, that is, to diminish the total sum of human lives by fifty years, was criminal. But to diminish the sum of human lives by fifty million years was not considered criminal. Isn't that absurd? Today, any ten-year-old will solve this mathematical-moral problem in half a minute. They, with all their Kants taken together, could not solve it (because it never occurred to any of the Kants to build a system of scientific ethics, i.e., ethics based on subtraction, addition, division, and multiplication).

commet: Both translations contain mistakes and let me make two lists of these mistakes: 1st translation: 1) The beginning of the sentence "With us, this mathematical-moral problem can be solved in half a minute" is a result of a word-by-word translation and as a result, the phrase sounds weird 2nd translation: 1) The phrase "no matter how limited their intelligence" lacks predicate 2) The ending of the phrase "such a way of life was truly mass murder芒聙聰even if slow murder" doesn't make any sense. text1: I didn芒聙聶t think so then, but the germs of those thoughts were already in me. I understood firstly that for all our wisdom my position alongside Schopenhauer and Solomon was a stupid one: we understand that life is evil and still we live. This is clearly stupid because if life is stupid芒聙聰and I do so love all that is rational芒聙聰then I should clearly destroy life, and no one would be able to challenge this. Secondly I understood that all our reasoning was going around in a vicious circle, like a wheel that has come off its gear. However much, however well we reason, we cannot give an answer to the question, and it will always be 0 = 0, and so our path is likely to be the wrong one. Thirdly, I began to understand that the answers given to faith enshrine the most profound wisdom of mankind, and that I didn芒聙聶t have the right to deny them on the grounds of reason, and that, most importantly, these answers do answer the question of life.

text1: He was accompanied, first, by Lebedev芒聙聶s nephew, already known to the reader, and, second, by Ippolit. Ippolit was a very young man, about seventeen, or perhaps eighteen, with an intelligent but constantly irritated expression on his face, on which illness had left its terrible marks. He was thin as a skeleton, pale yellow, his eyes glittered, and two red spots burned on his cheeks. He coughed incessantly; his every word, almost every breath, was accompanied by wheezing. A rather advanced stage of consumption was evident. It seemed that he had no more than two or three weeks left to live. He was very tired, and sank into a chair before anyone else. The rest made some show of ceremony on entering and were all but abashed, though they looked grave and were obviously afraid of somehow losing their dignity, which was strangely out of harmony with their reputation as negators of all useless social trivialities, prejudices, and almost everything in the world except their own interests.

commet: It is difficult to choose between these two as there are certain details I like in both translations, for example a small detail - mention of waves by the author of the second translation in the phrase "with the effect of a torpedo disappears into the waves" - it is an exact translation of the phrase from the source text. The same phrase in the first translation is still correct yet not as full. However as you may see I've chosen the first translation: 1) According to the source text Babichev is compared to an "idol" as in the first translation. but not "idiot" as in the second translation. I believe it to be a big mistake. 2) The phrase " He oppresses me" from the first translation conveys the exact meaning of the phrase from the source text. The same phrase " He crushes me" in the second translation isn't just that accurate

text1: After these words, of course, Aglaya immediately set off after the others, which, however, she had intended to do without that. Prince Shch., who was sitting with Adelaida, at her request immediately agreed to accompany the ladies. He had been interested in the prince even before, at the beginning of his acquaintance with the Yepanchins, when he had heard from them about him. It turned out that he knew him, that they had met somewhere recently and had been living together for two weeks in some little town. It was about three months ago. Prince Shch. even told a lot about the prince and generally spoke of him very sympathetically, so that now he went to visit his old acquaintance with sincere pleasure. General Ivan Fyodorovich was not at home this time. Yevgeny Pavlovich had not yet arrived either.
text2: After these words Aglaya naturally set out at once after them all, as she had intended to do in any event. Prince Shch., who was sitting with Adelaida, at her request immediately agreed to accompany the ladies. Still earlier, at the beginning of his acquaintance with the Epanchins, he had been extremely interested when he heard about the prince from them. It turned out that he was acquainted with him, that they had become acquainted not long ago and had lived together for a couple of weeks in the same little town. That was about three months ago. Prince Shch. had even told them a good deal about the prince and generally spoke of him with great sympathy, so that now it was with genuine pleasure that he went to visit his old acquaintance. General Ivan Fyodorovich was not at home at the time. Evgeny Pavlovich also had not arrived yet.

text1: And I remembered the whole course of my mental labors and I was horrified. It was now clear to me that for a man to be able to live he either had not to see the infinite or have an explanation of the meaning of life in which the finite was equated with the infinite. I had such an explanation but I had no need for it while I believed in the finite, and I began to test it by reason. And with the light of reason I found the whole of my previous explanation to dissolve in dust. But there came a time when I stopped believing in the finite. And then I began to construct out of what I knew, on rational foundations, an explanation that would give the meaning of life; but nothing got constructed. Together with mankind芒聙聶s best minds I came to 0 = 0 and was very surprised to get such a solution when nothing else could come of it.

text1: The prince was indeed looking at her very attentively at that moment, noticing that she had again begun to blush terribly. On such occasions, the more she blushed, the more she seemed to be angry with herself for it, which was evidently expressed in her flashing eyes; a moment later she would already be transferring her anger to the person with whom she was speaking, whether he was to blame or not, and would begin to quarrel with him. Knowing and feeling her own wildness and bashfulness, she usually entered into conversation little and was more silent than the other sisters, sometimes even too silent. When, especially in such delicate cases, it was absolutely necessary to speak, she would begin the conversation with unusual haughtiness and as if with some sort of challenge. She always had a presentiment in advance when she was about to begin or wanted to begin to blush.
text2: The prince was indeed peering at her intently at that moment, noticing that she had again begun to blush terribly. On such occasions, the more she blushed, the more she seemed to be angry with herself for it, as showed clearly in her flashing eyes; usually she would transfer her wrath a moment later to the one she was talking with, whether or not it was his fault, and begin to quarrel with him. Knowing and feeling her wildness and shyness, she usually entered little into conversation and was more taciturn than the other sisters, sometimes even much too taciturn. When, especially on such ticklish occasions, she absolutely had to speak, she would begin the conversation with an extraordinary haughtiness and as if with a sort of defiance. She always felt beforehand when she was beginning or about to begin to blush.

commet: These translations look like identical twins but the first one is still a little bit better. According to the source text the last sentence is more accurate in the first translation with the detail "when she was about to begin or WANTED to begin to blush". The same sentence in the second translation lacks this verb which is used in the source text and is surely important.

text1: The prince, however, heard that he had been called an idiot, and gave a start, but not because he had been called an idiot. The 芒聙聹idiot芒聙?he forgot at once. But in the crowd, not far from where he was sitting, somewhere to the side芒聙聰he would not have been able to show in what precise place and in what spot芒聙聰a face flashed, a pale face with dark, curly hair, with a familiar, a very familiar, smile and gaze芒聙聰flashed and disappeared. It might well have been that he only imagined it; of the whole apparition he was left with the impression of the crooked smile, the eyes, and the pale green, foppish tie that the gentleman who flashed was wearing. Whether this gentleman disappeared in the crowd or slipped into the vauxhall, the prince also could not have determined.

text1: It would seem that the prince芒聙聶s conversation was the most simple; but the simpler it was, the more absurd it became in the present case, and the experienced valet could not help feeling something that was perfectly proper between servant and servant, but perfectly improper between a guest and a servant. text2: It would seem that the prince's conversation was the simplest; but the simpler it was, the more absurd it became in this particular case, and an experienced valet could not help feeling something that was quite appropriate for a man with a man and quite inappropriate for a guest with a man. And since people are much smarter than their masters usually think them, it occurred to the valet that there were two possibilities: either the prince was a lackey of some sort and had come to ask for a job, or the prince was simply a fool and had no ambitions because an intelligent prince would never sit in the front hall and talk about his affairs with a lackey, and therefore, in both cases, he would not be responsible for him.
